metadata.title: Add per-IP rate limiting to the login endpoint
metadata.summary: Adds an in-memory token-bucket limiter and applies it to /auth/login. The goal is to slow down credential-stuffing attacks without affecting legitimate users. The limiter is intentionally simple — a single-process in-memory dict — because we only need short-term throttling and we already shed traffic at the load balancer for sustained abuse.

overview.purpose: A small, in-memory token-bucket rate limiter. You ask it `allow(key)` and it returns True or False based on how many requests `key` has made recently.
overview.background: A token bucket is a standard rate-limiting pattern: each key has a bucket that refills at a fixed rate up to a max capacity. Each request consumes one token; if the bucket is empty, the request is rejected. Compared to fixed-window counters, it gives smoother behavior at the boundary between windows.

keyPieces.name: TokenBucket
keyPieces.description: Holds the bucket state for a single key (current tokens + last-refill timestamp).

keyPieces.name: RateLimiter.allow
keyPieces.description: Refills the relevant bucket based on elapsed time, then consumes a token if one is available.

files.newContent:
"""In-memory token-bucket rate limiter.

Intentionally simple: a dict of buckets keyed by a caller-supplied string
(typically a client IP). Not safe across processes — see the module docstring
in `auth.py` for why that's fine for our use case.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import threading
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    tokens: float
    last_refill: float


class RateLimiter:
    def __init__(self, capacity: int, refill_per_second: float) -> None:
        self._capacity = capacity
        self._refill_per_second = refill_per_second
        self._buckets: dict[str, TokenBucket] = {}
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def allow(self, key: str) -> bool:
        now = time.monotonic()
        with self._lock:
            bucket = self._buckets.get(key)
            if bucket is None:
                bucket = TokenBucket(tokens=self._capacity, last_refill=now)
                self._buckets[key] = bucket

            elapsed = now - bucket.last_refill
            bucket.tokens = min(
                self._capacity,
                bucket.tokens + elapsed * self._refill_per_second,
            )
            bucket.last_refill = now

            if bucket.tokens >= 1.0:
                bucket.tokens -= 1.0
                return True
            return False

explanations.title: TokenBucket dataclass
explanations.what: Defines the per-key state: how many tokens are left and when we last refilled.
explanations.why: Pulled into a tiny dataclass so the bucket math reads cleanly. `tokens` is a float because partial tokens accumulate between requests as time passes.
explanations.impact: Each tracked key (client IP) holds two floats. With ~10k unique IPs in a hot window that's well under a megabyte of state.

explanations.title: Constructor + bucket store
explanations.what: Holds two policy knobs (capacity + refill rate) and a dict of buckets, plus a lock for thread safety.
explanations.why: Capacity sets how big a burst is allowed; refill rate sets the long-run sustained rate. The lock matters because Flask serves requests on multiple threads — without it, two threads could both decrement `tokens` from the same bucket and let a request through that should have been blocked.

explanations.title: allow(): the core rate-limit check
explanations.what: Looks up (or creates) the bucket for `key`, refills it based on elapsed time, then consumes one token if at least one is available.
explanations.why: This is the standard token-bucket algorithm. Computing the refill lazily — only when we're called — avoids a background timer thread and keeps the limiter accurate even after long idle periods.
explanations.impact: The `min(capacity, ...)` cap is important: without it, an idle bucket would accumulate unbounded tokens and the first burst could be huge. With it, the worst-case burst is exactly `capacity` requests.

overview.purpose: The HTTP authentication endpoints. Today that's just /auth/login; eventually it'll also host /auth/logout and the password-reset flow.
overview.background: We use Flask. `request.remote_addr` gives us the client IP after the load balancer rewrites it (the X-Forwarded-For handling lives in middleware, not here).

keyPieces.name: login()
keyPieces.description: Validates credentials and returns a session token, or 401 on failure.

files.newContent:
from flask import Blueprint, jsonify, request

from src.rate_limiter import RateLimiter
from src.users import verify_credentials, issue_session_token

auth_bp = Blueprint("auth", __name__)

# 5 attempts per IP, refilling at 1 attempt every 30 seconds.
# That's a hard cap of 5 in a burst, then ~2/minute sustained.
_login_limiter = RateLimiter(capacity=5, refill_per_second=1 / 30)


@auth_bp.post("/auth/login")
def login():
    client_ip = request.remote_addr or "unknown"
    if not _login_limiter.allow(client_ip):
        return jsonify({"error": "too many attempts, slow down"}), 429

    payload = request.get_json(force=True) or {}
    username = payload.get("username", "")
    password = payload.get("password", "")

    user = verify_credentials(username, password)
    if user is None:
        return jsonify({"error": "invalid credentials"}), 401

    token = issue_session_token(user.id)
    return jsonify({"token": token}), 200

explanations.title: Import the limiter
explanations.what: Pulls in the new RateLimiter class.
explanations.why: Kept as a sibling import because both modules live under `src/`.

explanations.title: Module-level limiter instance
explanations.what: Creates a single shared limiter for the whole module, configured for 5 attempts per IP with a 30-second refill.
explanations.why: Module-level so the dict of buckets persists across requests. Recreating it per request would reset all counters and defeat the whole point.
explanations.impact: Because state lives on the module object, restarting the process resets all limits. That's acceptable: if an attacker can trigger an app restart they have bigger problems, and our deploys are infrequent.

explanations.title: Rate-limit gate at the top of login()
explanations.what: Resolves the client IP and rejects with HTTP 429 if the bucket is empty.
explanations.why: Putting this BEFORE credential verification is deliberate. We don't want repeated bad logins to cost a bcrypt hash check — that's expensive enough to be a DoS amplifier on its own.
explanations.impact: 429 is the right status code for rate limits (vs. 401 for bad creds, vs. 403 for forbidden). Our load balancer dashboards already alert on 429 spikes.

overview.purpose: Integration tests for the auth blueprint. Hits the Flask test client and asserts on responses.
overview.background: We use pytest. The `client` fixture is a Flask test client wired up in conftest.py; it shares the same app object across the test run.

explanations.title: Auto-reset fixture for the limiter
explanations.what: An autouse fixture that replaces the module-level limiter with a fresh one before each test.
explanations.why: Because the limiter is module-level (see auth.py), state leaks across tests by default. Without this reset, the order tests run in would change which ones get rate-limited.
explanations.impact: Reaching into `auth_module._login_limiter` is a bit ugly — the underscore signals it's private — but the alternative (dependency injection through Flask config) felt like a lot of plumbing for one test seam.

explanations.title: New test: 6th attempt is rate-limited
explanations.what: Sends 5 bad logins, asserts each returns 401, then sends a 6th and asserts it returns 429.
explanations.why: Locks in the contract: capacity is 5 and the limiter sits in front of credential verification. If someone later moves the gate after the verify step (or bumps the capacity without thinking), this test fails.
explanations.impact: Note that this test depends on the 30-second refill being slow enough that no token comes back during the test. If we ever speed up refill in production, we'll need to inject a fake clock here instead.
